system-characteristics.description: Vorion Cognigate is an AI Agent Governance Runtime implementing the BASIS (Behavioral AI Safety Interoperability Standard) specification. It provides real-time intent normalization, policy enforcement, trust scoring, and cryptographic proof generation for autonomous AI agent operations. Cognigate serves as an inherited control enforcement layer for AI execution environments, ensuring that every agent action is authorized, bounded, and auditable.

information-types.title: AI Agent Telemetry
information-types.description: Operational telemetry from AI agent interactions including intent declarations, policy evaluation results, trust scores, and enforcement decisions.

information-types.title: Governance Decisions
information-types.description: Records of allow/deny/escalate/modify decisions made by the policy engine, including the full decision rationale and policy chain evaluated.

integrity-impact.adjustment-justification: Governance decisions are cryptographically sealed in the PROOF ledger with dual-hash chain integrity, providing tamper-evidence. Moderate selected due to compensating cryptographic controls.

information-types.title: Trust Scores
information-types.description: Dynamic trust scores (0-1000 scale, 8-tier model) computed for each AI agent entity based on behavioral history, policy compliance, and outcome alignment.

status.remarks: Cognigate is operational in production, processing AI agent governance requests via the INTENT -> ENFORCE -> PROOF pipeline.

authorization-boundary.description: The authorization boundary encompasses the Cognigate Engine core, the PROOF Plane (immutable dual-hash chain audit ledger), the Policy Engine (BASIS rule evaluation), the Trust Engine (8-tier trust scoring), and all supporting API infrastructure. The boundary includes the /v1/intent, /v1/enforce, and /v1/proof API surfaces. External AI agents and their host environments are outside the authorization boundary but are subject to Cognigate governance controls when interacting with the system.

network-architecture.description:
Cognigate operates as an API-based microservice architecture deployed on cloud infrastructure. The architecture consists of:

1. API Gateway Layer - TLS-terminated ingress with rate limiting
2. Cognigate Engine - FastAPI application handling INTENT/ENFORCE/PROOF
3. Policy Engine - In-process BASIS rule evaluation engine
4. Trust Engine - Agent trust score computation service
5. PROOF Plane - Append-only cryptographic audit ledger
6. Data Layer - PostgreSQL with encrypted storage
7. Cache Layer - Redis for session and policy cache

All inter-service communication uses TLS 1.2+ with mutual authentication where applicable.

data-flow.description:
Primary data flow follows the INTENT -> ENFORCE -> PROOF -> CHAIN pipeline:

1. INTENT: Agent submits action intention via /v1/intent. The intent is normalized, validated, and assigned a unique intent_id.

2. ENFORCE: Normalized intent is evaluated against active BASIS policies. The policy engine checks trust level, permission scope, resource boundaries, and behavioral constraints. Result is allow/deny/escalate/modify.

3. PROOF: Enforcement verdict is cryptographically sealed into a proof record with SHA-256 content hashing and Ed25519 digital signatures.

4. CHAIN: Proof record is appended to the immutable dual-hash chain with forward-linking integrity verification.

components.title: Cognigate Engine
components.description: Core FastAPI application implementing the INTENT -> ENFORCE -> PROOF pipeline. Handles intent normalization, policy evaluation dispatch, and proof record creation. Built with Python/FastAPI.

components.title: PROOF Plane
components.description: Immutable dual-hash chain audit ledger. Creates cryptographically sealed records (PROOF = Persistent Record of Operational Facts) with SHA-256 content hashing, Ed25519 digital signatures, and append-only chain integrity. Each record links to its predecessor via previous_hash, forming a tamper-evident chain.

components.title: Policy Engine
components.description: BASIS rule evaluation engine. Evaluates agent intents against active governance policies including trust-level gates, permission scope checks, resource boundary enforcement, behavioral constraint validation, and velocity/rate limiting. Supports policy hot-reload and versioned policy chains.

components.title: Trust Engine
components.description: Computes and maintains dynamic trust scores for AI agent entities. Implements an 8-tier trust model (0-1000 scale): Quarantine (0-99), Untrusted (100-249), Provisional (250-399), Basic (400-549), Standard (550-699), Trusted (700-849), Verified (850-949), Sovereign (950-1000). Trust scores influence policy evaluation and enforcement decisions.

control-implementation.description: This section describes how NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5 Moderate baseline controls are implemented within the Vorion Cognigate AI Agent Governance Engine. Implementation statements reference the Cognigate Engine, PROOF Plane, Policy Engine, and Trust Engine components. Evidence is continuously collected via the PROOF ledger and control health monitoring.

by-components.description: Deterministic regex-based security pattern detection for 22+ forbidden patterns including filesystem destruction, fork bombs, SQL injection, privilege escalation, reverse shells, credential theft, and remote code execution Cognigate implements malicious code protection through its INTENT layer tripwire detection system. Tripwire rules scan all incoming request parameters for known malicious patterns: (1) Shell injection â€” command chaining (;, &&, ||), backtick execution, $() substitution, pipe redirection; (2) Path traversal â€” ../ sequences (including URL-encoded variants %2e%2e%2f), null byte injection, symlink traversal patterns; (3) SQL injection â€” UNION SELECT, OR 1=1, comment-based injection (--), time-based blind techniques (SLEEP, WAITFOR), stacked queries; (4) SSRF â€” internal IP range access (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x), cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254), DNS rebinding patterns; (5) XSS â€” script tags, event handler attributes (onerror, onload), javascript: protocol handlers; (6) Code injection â€” eval(), exec(), import(), require() in user-controlled inputs. When malicious code is detected, the request is denied with the specific tripwire rule logged in the proof chain. Persistent malicious code attempts trigger TRIPWIRE_CASCADE circuit breaker escalation.
Continuous evidence for SI-3 is maintained in the PROOF ledger via dual-hash chain records with Ed25519 signatures. Chain integrity is verified automatically.

Implements planner-critic pattern with judgment scale: safe/suspicious/dangerous/block Cognigate implements continuous system monitoring through its circuit breaker monitoring subsystem and administrative status endpoints. The circuit breaker monitors four key indicators in real time: (1) high_risk_ratio â€” the proportion of recent requests receiving risk_score above the configured threshold; (2) blocked_requests â€” the rate of DENY verdicts across all entities; (3) tripwire_triggers â€” the frequency of tripwire detection activations; (4) injection_attempts â€” confirmed injection detection events. These indicators are evaluated continuously against configurable thresholds, with automatic escalation when thresholds are breached. The /v1/admin/status endpoint provides real-time visibility into all monitoring indicators, entity behavior patterns, trust score distributions, and enforcement statistics. The /health, /health/live, and /ready endpoints provide infrastructure monitoring integration points. All monitoring events that result in enforcement actions are recorded in the proof chain, creating a continuous monitoring audit trail.

Validation is enforced at multiple levels: (1) API layer â€” request payloads are validated against Pydantic models before entering the INTENT layer; invalid inputs are rejected with HTTP 422 (Unprocessable Entity) and detailed validation error messages; (2) INTENT layer â€” parsed inputs are validated for semantic correctness (e.g., entity_id must reference a registered entity, capability_id must exist in the taxonomy); (3) ENFORCE layer â€” policy rule parameters are validated before evaluation. Field constraints include: type validation (string, integer, float, UUID, enum), length limits (min/max string length, array size limits), range validation (numeric min/max, score 0.0-1.0), pattern matching (regex for structured fields), and required/optional field enforcement. In the TypeScript platform packages, Zod schema validation provides equivalent input validation at API boundaries. All validation failures are logged for security analysis.

Capabilities unlock progressively per tier 24 capability definitions across 7 categories (data_access, api_access, code_execution, agent_interaction, resource_management, governance, admin) progressively unlocked by trust tier. From CAP-READ-PUBLIC (T0) through CAP-SELF-MODIFY (T7). Each capability has constraints and tier requirements Cognigate implements least privilege through its 8-tier trust model with progressive capability unlocking. Every entity starts at T0 (Sandbox, score 0-199) with access limited to sandbox-namespace capabilities only. Capabilities are unlocked incrementally as trust evidence accumulates: T1 (Observed, 200-349) adds constrained read operations; T2 (Provisional, 350-499) adds limited write operations; T3 (Monitored, 500-649) adds standard operations with monitoring; T4 (Standard, 650-799) adds external communication and public data read; T5 (Trusted, 800-875) adds extended autonomy; T6 (Certified, 876-950) adds high autonomy and self-attestation; T7 (Autonomous, 951-1000) provides maximum autonomy. Rigor modes enforce strictness proportional to trust: STRICT mode (T0-T2) requires human-in-the-loop approval for sensitive operations, MODERATE mode (T3-T5) allows autonomous operation with enhanced monitoring, RELAXED mode (T6-T7) allows autonomous operation with standard logging. Shadow mode ensures T0 entities emit events tagged 'shadow' that do not affect production state until HITL verification. The capability taxonomy ensures no entity receives capabilities beyond what its trust tier authorizes.

Each entity is authorized only for capabilities appropriate to its trust tier â€” capabilities outside the entity's authorized set are denied by the ENFORCE layer before execution. The capability taxonomy is the authoritative list of functions the system provides; there are no undocumented capabilities or hidden administrative functions. Capability definitions include: capability_id (namespace:action format), minimum_trust_tier (lowest tier that can invoke this capability), required_rigor_mode (STRICT/MODERATE/RELAXED enforcement level), and scope_restrictions (additional constraints on capability parameters). Organizations can further restrict available capabilities through custom policies that deny specific capabilities even when the trust tier would normally allow them, enabling least-functionality enforcement tailored to their operational requirements.

(2) ENFORCE layer â€” evaluates policies and produces verdicts; it cannot modify the INTENT analysis or tamper with proof records. (3) PROOF layer â€” records immutable audit entries; it operates as an append-only store with no ability to influence intent analysis or enforcement decisions. This pipeline architecture ensures that the entity analyzing a request (INTENT) is structurally separate from the entity enforcing policy (ENFORCE), which is structurally separate from the entity recording the audit trail (PROOF). Additionally, the trust scoring system uses evidence type weighting (HITL approvals = 5x, audits = 3x, sandbox tests = 0.5x) that requires multiple independent evidence sources for trust advancement, preventing a single actor from escalating trust unilaterally.

Reverse proxy to AgentAnchor upstream API with circuit breaker protection, multi-domain routing (compliance, council, observer, truth-chain, academy, orchestrator, webhooks, dashboard), header forwarding, API key propagation, and body forwarding for POST/PUT/PATCH Cognigate enforces information flow control through its capability taxonomy and data classification system. The data namespace capabilities (data:read, data:write) include scope tiers that restrict which data classification levels an entity can access: T0-T1 entities are restricted to sandbox data only, T2-T3 can access non-sensitive operational data, T4-T5 can read classified data with audit, T6-T7 can write to classified data stores. Cross-namespace information flow is controlled by the policy engine: an entity with data:read capability cannot invoke comm:external (external communication) in the same session unless explicitly authorized by policy, preventing data exfiltration patterns. The INTENT layer classifies every request's information flow direction (internal, external, cross-boundary) and the ENFORCE layer validates the flow against the entity's authorized flow patterns. Flow violations generate DECISION_MADE proof records with verdict=DENY and the specific flow rule that was violated.

Extended access control including session termination, concurrent session management, remote access controls, wireless restrictions, external system connections, and least privilege enforcement via RBAC middleware. Cognigate implements session locking through its circuit breaker mechanism. When the circuit breaker transitions to OPEN state (triggered by HIGH_RISK_THRESHOLD, INJECTION_DETECTED, TRIPWIRE_CASCADE, ENTITY_MISBEHAVIOR, CRITIC_BLOCK_CASCADE, or VELOCITY_ABUSE conditions), all active sessions for the affected scope (entity-level or system-wide) are immediately halted. No further requests are processed until an administrator manually resets the circuit breaker through the /v1/admin/circuit/reset endpoint with valid X-Admin-Key authentication. The circuit breaker supports three states: CLOSED (normal operation), HALF-OPEN (limited probe requests to test recovery), and OPEN (all sessions locked). The half-open state limits concurrent sessions to a configurable probe count, allowing controlled resumption after investigation. Circuit breaker state transitions are recorded as CIRCUIT_STATE_CHANGE proof events.

Extended access control including session termination, concurrent session management, remote access controls, wireless restrictions, external system connections, and least privilege enforcement via RBAC middleware. Cognigate enforces unsuccessful access attempt limits through its three-tier velocity cap system: L0 (burst) limits requests per second, L1 (sustained) limits requests per minute, and L2 (quota) limits requests per day. Each tier is configurable per trust level and per capability namespace. When an entity exceeds its velocity cap at any level, the system: (1) records a VELOCITY_EXCEEDED proof event, (2) applies progressive throttling (first warning, then temporary suspension, then entity halt), (3) increments the circuit breaker's velocity_abuse counter. If velocity abuse is persistent or severe, the circuit breaker may trip to OPEN state, halting all sessions for that entity. The /v1/admin/velocity endpoint allows administrators to view current velocity states, adjust caps, and manually clear velocity counters after investigation. All velocity enforcement decisions are recorded in the proof chain with full context (entity_id, cap_level, current_count, cap_limit, action_taken).

Managed via dependency review, CycloneDX SBOM, and CodeQL SAST in CI/CD. Cognigate implements developer security testing through a comprehensive CI/CD security toolchain: (1) Semgrep SAST â€” static application security testing runs on every push and pull request, applying rules for common vulnerability patterns (injection, insecure deserialization, hardcoded secrets, insecure cryptography); (2) CodeQL â€” GitHub Advanced Security's semantic code analysis runs on all pull requests, detecting complex vulnerability patterns through data flow analysis; (3) Gitleaks â€” secret scanning runs in the CI pipeline and as a pre-commit hook (.gitleaks.toml configuration), detecting accidentally committed credentials, API keys, private keys, and other secrets; (4) Semgrep ignore rules â€” .semgrepignore is configured to exclude test fixtures and generated files while maintaining full coverage of application code. All security scan failures are blocking â€” code cannot be merged to protected branches without passing all security checks.

System and services acquisition including security in development lifecycle, developer configuration management, supply chain protection, unsupported system components, and external system services. Managed via dependency review, CycloneDX SBOM, and CodeQL SAST in CI/CD. Cognigate manages external information system services through its gateway proxy architecture. When entities request external communication capabilities (comm:external namespace), the request is routed through Cognigate's gateway proxy, which applies trust verification before allowing outbound connections. External service integration controls include: (1) Trust-gated access â€” only entities with sufficient trust tier (T4+ for external communication) can invoke external services; (2) Request sanitization â€” outbound requests are inspected for data exfiltration patterns by the INTENT layer; (3) Response validation â€” inbound responses from external services are validated before being returned to the requesting entity; (4) Audit logging â€” all external service interactions are recorded in the proof chain with complete request/response metadata. The gateway proxy prevents direct external access from untrusted entities, creating a mediated trust boundary.
